Notes: Distinctive squarrose seed heads. Forms dense clumps in moist shady areas. Useful for wet woodland edges and stream banks where few other sedges thrive in shade.
seed_notes: Squarrose spikes with spreading scales; shake when dry
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring; easy

Notes: Dramatic hot-pink warty fruit capsules split open in fall to reveal orange-red seeds — one of the most visually striking fall displays of any native shrub. Shade tolerant understory shrub.
seed_notes: Striking pink capsules split to reveal orange-red seeds; collect fresh; cold stratify
propagation_notes: Divide suckers in early spring; tip layering works — pin stem to ground in spring, sever following year

Notes: Enormous dinner-plate-sized flowers in white or pink — the most dramatic native perennial for wet sites. Hummingbird plant. Dies back to the ground each year but regrows reliably.
seed_notes: Large dry capsules; collect when brown
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring; also easy from seed (scar seed coat with sandpaper)

Notes: Upper salt marsh shrub that dominates the high tide line along coastal MA. Tolerates salt spray, periodic flooding, and poor soils. Important habitat structure for marsh wrens and coastal birds. Primarily relevant for coastal and estuarine planting projects.
seed_notes: Small dry achenes; strip from dry stems
propagation_notes: Division or hardwood cuttings in late winter; tolerates harsh conditions once established

Notes: Classic wetland rush forming dense upright tufts. Important structure for wetland plantings and rain gardens. Seeds eaten by birds and waterfowl.
seed_notes: Small round capsules in clusters; collect when brown
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring; very easy

Notes: Elegant nodding yellow-orange bells on tall stems. Hummingbird and swallowtail butterfly plant. Requires consistent moisture. One of the showiest native perennials for wet meadow edges.
seed_notes: Dry capsules with many seeds; collect when brown and papery
propagation_notes: Divide bulb scales or offsets in fall; slow to establish — be patient

Notes: The tallest native lily — can reach 8 feet with dozens of dramatic recurved orange flowers spotted with purple. Hummingbird magnet. Requires rich moist soil. Spectacular in a rain garden or wet meadow.
seed_notes: Dry capsules with many seeds; collect when brown
propagation_notes: Divide bulb scales or offsets in fall; slow to establish

Notes: Delicate blue-lavender bell-shaped flowers in spring on ladder-like pinnate foliage. Native to eastern North America. Excellent shade perennial that combines well with ferns and woodland groundcovers. Self-seeds gently without becoming weedy.
seed_notes: Small capsules; collect when brown before they shatter; self-seeds gently
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring; also self-seeds reliably — allow seedlings to fill in

Notes: Deep rose-pink flower spikes. Woolly undersides on leaves. Prefers wetter conditions. Good companion to Joe-Pye weed and native grasses in wet meadow plantings.

Notes: Large showy magenta-pink flowers over a long season — more ornamental than fruiting. Thornless canes with attractive shredding bark. Spreads by suckers to form colonies. Shade tolerant and adaptable. Often mislabeled at nurseries as Canada raspberry. Fruit is dry and not particularly edible but birds use it.
seed_notes: Small dry drupelets; collect when red; clean pulp; cold stratify
propagation_notes: Divide suckers in early spring; spreads readily by root suckers. Tip layering also works.

Notes: Fast-growing native willow that colonizes disturbed ground, roadsides, and wet areas readily. Early catkins are among the first nectar sources of spring. Documented naturalizing in dry compacted urban sites including traffic islands — more adaptable than literature suggests. Forms thickets; good for erosion control and habitat patches.
seed_notes: Cottony seeds disperse very quickly — collect immediately when capsules begin to open
propagation_notes: Live staking works extremely well — one of the easiest plants to live stake. Cut pencil-thick stems in late winter, push 2/3 into moist soil before leaves emerge. Also spreads readily by suckering.
